pregnancy_stages.title: First Trimester Milestones
pregnancy_stages.content: Heart begins beating ~6 weeks; limbs form by 8 weeks.

pregnancy_stages.title: Second Trimester Milestones
pregnancy_stages.content: Vernix, lanugo; mother feels fetal movement 18–20 weeks.

pregnancy_stages.title: Third Trimester Overview
pregnancy_stages.content: Weeks 28–40: fetal maturation, maternal discomfort increases.

pregnancy_stages.title: Third Trimester Milestones
pregnancy_stages.content: Lung surfactant matures; betamethasone 24–34 weeks if preterm risk.

pregnancy_stages.title: Prenatal Visit Schedule
pregnancy_stages.content: Monthly until 28 weeks, q2 weeks until 36, weekly until delivery.
nursing_actions: BP, weight, urine protein, fundal height

pregnancy_stages.title: Fetal Well-Being Assessment
pregnancy_stages.content: Kick counts, NST, BPP when indicated for high-risk.

labor_delivery.title: Stage 1 Labor
labor_delivery.content: Onset of contractions to full dilation; active labor ≥6 cm; transition ~8–10 cm intense.

labor_delivery.title: FHR Baseline Assessment
labor_delivery.content: Normal 110–160 bpm; variability moderate; accelerations reassuring.

labor_delivery.title: FHR Decelerations
labor_delivery.content: Early: mirror contractions. Variable: cord compression. Late: gradual nadir after contraction peak — uteroplacental insufficiency.
nursing_actions: Late decels: stop oxytocin, left lateral, O₂, IV fluids, notify provider

labor_delivery.title: Leopold Maneuvers
labor_delivery.content: Palpate fetal lie, presentation, and position.

labor_delivery.title: Labor Positioning
labor_delivery.content: Upright positions aid descent; left lateral improves placental perfusion.
nursing_actions: Birth ball, squat bar as available

postpartum_newborn.title: Postpartum Bubble Assessment
postpartum_newborn.content: B — bonding; U — uterus firm/fundal height; B — breasts; B — bowel/bladder; L — lochia; E — episiotomy/edema.

postpartum_newborn.title: Lochia Assessment
postpartum_newborn.content: Rubra days 1–3; serosa 4–10; alba up to 6 weeks. Foul odor or heavy bleeding abnormal.

postpartum_newborn.title: Postpartum Maternal Warning Signs
postpartum_newborn.content: Fever, foul lochia, incision erythema, chest pain, leg swelling, mood changes with hallucinations.

postpartum_newborn.title: APGAR Scoring
postpartum_newborn.content: 1 and 5 minutes: Appearance, Pulse, Grimace, Activity, Respiration. <7 needs intervention.

postpartum_newborn.title: Immediate Newborn Care
postpartum_newborn.content: Dry, warm, stimulate, delay cord clamp per policy, skin-to-skin, vitamin K and eye prophylaxis per order.

postpartum_newborn.title: Newborn Physical Assessment
postpartum_newborn.content: Vitals, reflexes, skin, fontanelles, hips, heart/lung auscultation.

postpartum_newborn.title: Bonding & Kangaroo Care
postpartum_newborn.content: Skin-to-skin stabilizes temperature and glucose; promotes attachment.

postpartum_newborn.title: Breastfeeding Basics
postpartum_newborn.content: Latch, frequency 8–12 feeds/24 hr, wet diapers day 4+.
nursing_actions: Assess latch and maternal nipple pain

postpartum_newborn.title: Newborn Warning Signs
postpartum_newborn.content: Poor feeding, lethargy, respiratory distress, circumoral cyanosis, fever ≥38°C.

pediatric_essentials.title: Growth Percentiles
pediatric_essentials.content: Weight, length, head circumference plotted on WHO/CDC charts.
nursing_actions: Trend over time more important than single point

pediatric_essentials.title: Infant Milestones
pediatric_essentials.content: Social smile ~2 mo, rolls ~4–6 mo, sits ~6 mo, crawls ~9 mo.

pediatric_essentials.title: Toddler Milestones
pediatric_essentials.content: Walks ~12 mo, 50 words ~18 mo, parallel play.

pediatric_essentials.title: Immunization Schedule
pediatric_essentials.content: CDC schedule — DTaP, IPV, MMR, varicella, Hep B series.

pediatric_essentials.title: Pediatric Fever
pediatric_essentials.content: Context matters — behavior, hydration, age. Infant ≤28 days with ≥38°C = emergency workup.

pediatric_essentials.title: Pediatric Dehydration
pediatric_essentials.content: Assess fontanel, mucous membranes, urine output, cap refill.
nursing_actions: Oral rehydration if mild; IV if severe

pediatric_essentials.title: Pediatric Pain Assessment
pediatric_essentials.content: FLACC infant; FACES/Wong-Baker child; self-report when able.

pediatric_essentials.title: Pediatric Vital Sign Norms — Infant (0–12 mo)
pediatric_essentials.content: HR 100–160, RR 30–60, BP ~70–100/50–65 systolic. Temperature: rectal most accurate in infants.
key_points: Bradycardia in infant often signals hypoxia — assess ABCs before assuming sleep
Cap refill >2 sec suggests poor perfusion
Pulse ox on right hand pre-ductal in newborn if concern for CHD
nursing_actions: Use length-based resuscitation tape (Broselow) for weight-based dosing
Trend vitals; single reading less meaningful than pattern
pediatric_essentials.clinical_why: Age-specific norms prevent mislabeling normal infant tachycardia as emergency or missing true decompensation.

pediatric_essentials.title: Pediatric Vital Sign Norms — Toddler (1–3 yr)
pediatric_essentials.content: HR 90–150, RR 24–40. Fear and crying elevate HR/RR — allow calm period before reassessment.
key_points: Communicate at eye level; use simple words
Parent presence reduces anxiety and improves cooperation
Weight in kg required for all pediatric medication calculations
nursing_actions: Explain procedures in developmentally appropriate language
Offer choices when safe (which arm for BP)
pediatric_essentials.clinical_why: Toddlers cannot articulate symptoms — behavioral cues and parent report are primary assessment data.

pediatric_essentials.title: Pediatric Vital Sign Norms — School Age (6–12 yr)
pediatric_essentials.content: HR 70–120, RR 18–30, BP approaching adult ranges by adolescence.
key_points: Children may minimize pain — observe gait, activity, facial expression
School-age children understand cause-effect; brief honest explanations build trust
nursing_actions: Involve child in care when appropriate
pediatric_essentials.clinical_why: Normal ranges widen with age — applying adult vital sign alarms causes false emergencies.

pediatric_essentials.title: Family-Centered & Developmental Communication
pediatric_essentials.content: Assess the child within the context of family; include caregivers in teaching. Use therapeutic touch, distraction, and play for younger children.
key_points: Infants: nonverbal cues (tone, touch, feeding)
Toddlers: short sentences, concrete terms, avoid 'don't hurt'
Adolescents: confidential time; assess risk behaviors respectfully
nursing_actions: Ask parent what calms the child
Use Child Life services when available
pediatric_essentials.clinical_why: NCLEX Psychosocial Integrity emphasizes family-centered care and age-appropriate interaction.

pediatric_essentials.title: SIDS Prevention & Safe Sleep
pediatric_essentials.content: ABC: Alone, on Back, in Crib. Firm flat surface, no soft bedding, smoke-free environment.
key_points: Supine sleeping every sleep period until 1 year
Room-sharing without bed-sharing recommended first 6 months
Pacifier at nap/bedtime after breastfeeding established
nursing_actions: Teach parents to avoid overlays, bumpers, inclined sleepers
pediatric_essentials.clinical_why: Safe sleep teaching is a high-yield NCLEX Health Promotion item linked to sudden unexpected infant death reduction.

pediatric_essentials.title: Choking & Foreign Body Aspiration
pediatric_essentials.content: Toddlers explore orally — coins, grapes, hot dogs are common aspirated objects. Partial obstruction may have stridor and cough; complete obstruction is silent with ineffective cough.
key_points: Infant: 5 back blows + 5 chest thrusts
Child >1 yr: abdominal thrusts (Heimlich)
Cut food to <½ inch; avoid round firm foods for toddlers
nursing_actions: Assess airway before any other intervention
Call code/respond per facility BLS protocol
pediatric_essentials.clinical_why: Airway obstruction is an immediate life threat — NCLEX prioritization favors airway interventions first.

pediatric_essentials.title: Pediatric Medication Safety
pediatric_essentials.content: Always verify weight in kg. Never use 'teaspoon' household measures. Liquid concentrations vary — read label every time.
key_points: mg/kg dosing — double-check with another nurse
Never administer adult tablets split without pharmacy approval
Topical medications absorb faster in infants — systemic toxicity risk
nursing_actions: Use oral syringe not kitchen spoon
Document site for topical meds; teach parents
pediatric_essentials.clinical_why: Pediatric dosing errors are a leading cause of preventable harm — dimensional analysis and weight verification are NCLEX staples.

pediatric_essentials.title: Lead Poisoning Screening
pediatric_essentials.content: CDC: screen all children at 12 and 24 months; high-risk areas may need earlier/more frequent screening.
key_points: Pica, developmental delay, irritability may present
Old paint, imported toys, water pipes are exposure sources
nursing_actions: Refer elevated BLL per local health department protocol
Teach handwashing and wet-mop dust control
pediatric_essentials.clinical_why: Environmental health screening is Health Promotion — anticipatory guidance prevents irreversible neurodevelopmental injury.

pediatric_essentials.title: Adolescent Health & Confidentiality
pediatric_essentials.content: Assess STI risk, substance use, depression, and safety confidentially when state law permits. Balance autonomy with mandatory reporting for abuse/harm.
key_points: HEADSS assessment: Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide
HPV, meningococcal, Tdap boosters per adolescent schedule
pediatric_essentials.clinical_why: Adolescents disclose sensitive information only when trust and privacy are protected.

pediatric_essentials.title: Croup (Laryngotracheobronchitis)
pediatric_essentials.content: Barking cough, inspiratory stridor, hoarseness — often worse at night. Mild: cool mist; severe: racemic epinephrine and steroids per order.
key_points: Stridor at rest = severe — continuous monitoring
Avoid agitating child; stress worsens obstruction
nursing_actions: Notify provider for stridor at rest or retractions
pediatric_essentials.clinical_why: Pediatric airway edema can progress rapidly — early escalation prevents respiratory arrest.

safety_red_flags.finding: BP ≥140/90 after 20 weeks with proteinuria OR systemic symptoms (headache, RUQ pain, vision changes)
safety_red_flags.action: Notify provider; assess HELLP; seizure precautions; magnesium per order
safety_red_flags.escalation: Magnesium sulfate for severe features; delivery may be indicated

safety_red_flags.action: Side-lying, airway, magnesium per protocol, notify provider, prepare for delivery

safety_red_flags.finding: Postpartum hemorrhage — fundus boggy, soaking >1 pad/hr
safety_red_flags.action: Fundal massage, IV access, oxytocin per order, notify provider, blood products

safety_red_flags.finding: Umbilical cord prolapse with fetal bradycardia
safety_red_flags.action: Knee-chest or exaggerated Sims; elevate presenting part; emergency cesarean

safety_red_flags.finding: Turtle sign — head delivered, shoulders stuck
safety_red_flags.action: McRoberts + suprapubic pressure; NO fundal pressure; notify team

safety_red_flags.finding: Painful rigid abdomen, dark bleeding, nonreassuring FHR
safety_red_flags.action: Left lateral, O₂, IV access, notify provider, prepare for emergency delivery

safety_red_flags.finding: Hallucinations, delusions, bizarre behavior days–4 weeks postpartum
safety_red_flags.action: Constant observation; infant safety; psychiatry; do not leave alone with baby
Full sepsis workup per protocol; notify provider; NPO for procedures

safety_red_flags.finding: Nasal flaring, grunting, retractions, circumoral cyanosis
Repetitive late decelerations with decreased variability
safety_red_flags.action: Stop oxytocin, left lateral, O₂, IV fluids, notify provider

safety_red_flags.finding: Absent DTRs, RR <12, urine output <30 mL/hr on magnesium
safety_red_flags.action: Stop magnesium; notify provider; calcium gluconate per order

safety_red_flags.finding: Fever, foul lochia, uterine tenderness postpartum
safety_red_flags.action: Cultures; antibiotics per order; monitor vitals

safety_red_flags.finding: Jaundice <24 hr of life or rapidly rising bilirubin
safety_red_flags.action: Bilirubin level; phototherapy per order; breastfeeding support

safety_red_flags.finding: Barking cough, stridor at rest in toddler
safety_red_flags.action: Comfort; nebulized epinephrine/steroids per order; airway monitoring

safety_red_flags.finding: Injuries inconsistent with history in child
safety_red_flags.action: Mandatory reporting; separate interviews; document objectively

complications_drill.finding: Sudden heavy lochia with boggy fundus 1 hr postpartum
options: Fundal massage and notify provider — PPH
complications_drill.explanation: Priority action: Fundal massage and notify provider — PPH
complications_drill.clinical_why: Maternal-fetal and neonatal safety prioritization per NCLEX.

complications_drill.finding: BP 168/112, RUQ pain, 3+ protein at 34 weeks
options: Preeclampsia protocol — notify provider, assess HELLP
complications_drill.explanation: Priority action: Preeclampsia protocol — notify provider, assess HELLP
complications_drill.clinical_why: Maternal-fetal and neonatal safety prioritization per NCLEX.

options: Stop oxytocin, intrauterine resuscitation, notify provider
complications_drill.explanation: Priority action: Stop oxytocin, intrauterine resuscitation, notify provider
complications_drill.clinical_why: Maternal-fetal and neonatal safety prioritization per NCLEX.

options: Knee-chest, elevate presenting part, emergency delivery prep
complications_drill.explanation: Priority action: Knee-chest, elevate presenting part, emergency delivery prep
complications_drill.clinical_why: Maternal-fetal and neonatal safety prioritization per NCLEX.

complications_drill.finding: Newborn axillary temp 38.5°C at 10 days old
options: Sepsis workup per neonatal fever protocol
complications_drill.explanation: Priority action: Sepsis workup per neonatal fever protocol
complications_drill.clinical_why: Maternal-fetal and neonatal safety prioritization per NCLEX.

complications_drill.finding: Mother reports auditory hallucinations 5 days postpartum
complications_drill.explanation: Priority action: 1:1, infant safety, psychiatry — postpartum psychosis
complications_drill.clinical_why: Maternal-fetal and neonatal safety prioritization per NCLEX.

options: Stop magnesium, notify provider, check RR and urine output
complications_drill.explanation: Priority action: Stop magnesium, notify provider, check RR and urine output
complications_drill.clinical_why: Maternal-fetal and neonatal safety prioritization per NCLEX.

complications_drill.explanation: Priority action: Emergency workup — infant ≤28 days standard
complications_drill.clinical_why: Maternal-fetal and neonatal safety prioritization per NCLEX.

complications_drill.finding: Variable decelerations with cord visible at introitus
options: Amnioinfusion per order or position change; prepare for cesarean if nonreassuring
complications_drill.explanation: Priority action: Amnioinfusion per order or position change; prepare for cesarean if nonreassuring
complications_drill.clinical_why: Maternal-fetal and neonatal safety prioritization per NCLEX.

options: Referral for developmental evaluation — not normal variant
complications_drill.explanation: Priority action: Referral for developmental evaluation — not normal variant
complications_drill.clinical_why: Maternal-fetal and neonatal safety prioritization per NCLEX.

options: McRoberts and suprapubic pressure — no fundal pressure
complications_drill.explanation: Priority action: McRoberts and suprapubic pressure — no fundal pressure
complications_drill.clinical_why: Maternal-fetal and neonatal safety prioritization per NCLEX.

complications_drill.finding: RUQ pain, platelets 68k, AST elevated postpartum with confusion
options: HELLP vs psychosis overlap — labs and psych eval; notify provider
complications_drill.explanation: Priority action: HELLP vs psychosis overlap — labs and psych eval; notify provider
complications_drill.clinical_why: Maternal-fetal and neonatal safety prioritization per NCLEX.

options: Intrauterine resuscitation before pushing — fetal status priority
complications_drill.explanation: Priority action: Intrauterine resuscitation before pushing — fetal status priority
complications_drill.clinical_why: Maternal-fetal and neonatal safety prioritization per NCLEX.

flashcards.back: Assess at 1 and 5 min — <7 needs help
≥140/90 after 20 wks with proteinuria or systemic symptoms
Uteroplacental insufficiency — stop pitocin, O₂, fluids
Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets + RUQ pain
Days 2–3 term infant; pathologic if <24 hr
Knee-chest preferred over Trendelenburg in pregnancy
Face Legs Activity Cry Consolability — infant pain
IM per protocol — prevents hemorrhagic disease
Sunken fontanel, dry mucosa, decreased urine
Turtle sign — head retracts against perineum
≤28 days with rectal temp ≥38°C (100.4°F) = full sepsis workup
Alone, on Back, in Crib — firm surface, no soft bedding
Rolls back→side, social smile, reaches for objects, improved head control
Walks with support/first steps, single words, object permanence
FLACC scale: Face, Legs, Activity, Cry, Consolability
Aspirin — Reye syndrome risk with viral illness
5 back blows + 5 chest thrusts — do not blind finger sweep
Airborne AND contact until lesions crusted (CDC)
Always kg; oral syringe; verify concentration (mg/mL)

practice_questions.question: Late decelerations during labor — first action?
practice_questions.explanation: Late decelerations are uniform, gradual decreases below baseline after contraction peak — uteroplacental insufficiency. Stop oxytocin, left lateral position, O₂, IV fluids, notify provider.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Persistent late decels with absent variability require fetal scalp stimulation or scalp pH/lactate and possible expedited delivery.

source.title: Open RN — Maternal-Newborn Nursing
source.citation: Open RN OER — Maternal-Newborn; NCSBN Health Promotion

practice_questions.question: Postpartum fundus boggy — intervene with:
practice_questions.explanation: Boggy uterus indicates atony — massage fundus firmly until firm, then assess lochia and VS. Prepare uterotonics (oxytocin, methylergonovine, carboprost) per order.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Postpartum hemorrhage from atony is leading cause of maternal mortality — first minutes determine blood loss.

source.title: Open RN — Maternal-Newborn Nursing
source.citation: Open RN OER — Maternal-Newborn; NCSBN Health Promotion

practice_questions.question: Preeclampsia severe feature includes:
practice_questions.explanation: Severe preeclampsia features: BP ≥160/110, platelets <100k, creatinine >1.1, pulmonary edema, RUQ/epigastric pain (liver capsule distension), cerebral symptoms.
practice_questions.clinical_why: RUQ pain may signal HELLP syndrome — hepatic rupture and DIC risk require magnesium and delivery planning.

source.title: Open RN — Maternal-Newborn Nursing
source.citation: Open RN OER — Maternal-Newborn; NCSBN Health Promotion

practice_questions.question: Shoulder dystocia maneuver:
practice_questions.explanation: McRoberts (hyperflex hips) and suprapubic pressure free the anterior shoulder; fundal pressure worsens impaction and is contraindicated.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Shoulder dystocia causes brachial plexus injury and asphyxia — mnemonic HELPERR guides sequential maneuvers.

source.title: Open RN — Maternal-Newborn Nursing
source.citation: Open RN OER — Maternal-Newborn; NCSBN Health Promotion

practice_questions.question: Newborn APGAR 4 at 1 minute — nurse:
options: Continue resuscitation per NRP and reassess at 5 min
practice_questions.explanation: APGAR 4 at 1 minute indicates moderate depression — continue PPV, stimulation, and NRP algorithm; reassess at 5 and 10 minutes.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Low 5-minute APGAR correlates with neonatal encephalopathy — document interventions for NICU handoff.

source.title: Open RN — Maternal-Newborn Nursing
source.citation: Open RN OER — Maternal-Newborn; NCSBN Health Promotion

practice_questions.question: Magnesium toxicity sign:
practice_questions.explanation: Magnesium toxicity: absent DTRs (first sign), RR <12, urine output <30 mL/hr, flushing — stop Mg, give calcium gluconate, support respirations.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Magnesium is antidote for eclampsia but narrow margin — therapeutic level 4–7 mEq/L; >10 can arrest breathing.

source.title: Open RN — Maternal-Newborn Nursing
source.citation: Open RN OER — Maternal-Newborn; NCSBN Health Promotion

practice_questions.question: 14-day-old with 38.3°C fever — priority:
options: Full sepsis evaluation per neonatal protocol
practice_questions.explanation: Fever ≥38°C (100.4°F) in infant ≤28 days requires full sepsis workup: blood culture, UA/UCx, LP, empiric IV antibiotics — never outpatient management alone.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Neonatal sepsis progresses rapidly — E. coli and GBS are common pathogens with high mortality if delayed.

source.title: Open RN — Maternal-Newborn Nursing
source.citation: Open RN OER — Maternal-Newborn; NCSBN Health Promotion

practice_questions.question: Cord prolapse nursing action:
options: Knee-chest, elevate presenting part, notify for emergency delivery
Push cord back into vagina repeatedly
practice_questions.explanation: Cord prolapse: call for help, knee-chest or exaggerated Trendelenburg, elevate presenting part off cord with sterile gloved hand, O₂, prepare emergency cesarean.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Fetal bradycardia from cord compression causes hypoxic-ischemic injury within minutes.

source.title: Open RN — Maternal-Newborn Nursing
source.citation: Open RN OER — Maternal-Newborn; NCSBN Health Promotion

practice_questions.question: Physiologic jaundice typically appears:
practice_questions.explanation: Physiologic jaundice peaks days 2–3 in term infants from immature hepatic conjugation; pathologic if <24 hr, rapid rise, or bilirubin crosses exchange transfusion thresholds.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Kernicterus from untreated hyperbilirubinemia causes permanent neurologic damage.

source.title: Open RN — Maternal-Newborn Nursing
source.citation: Open RN OER — Maternal-Newborn; NCSBN Health Promotion

practice_questions.question: Postpartum psychosis requires:
options: Immediate psychiatric care and infant safety measures
practice_questions.explanation: Postpartum psychosis (days to 4 weeks): delusions, hallucinations, bizarre behavior — psychiatric emergency; never leave mother alone with infant.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Infanticide and suicide risk are elevated — inpatient psychiatric care with infant safety planning.

source.title: Open RN — Maternal-Newborn Nursing
source.citation: Open RN OER — Maternal-Newborn; NCSBN Health Promotion

practice_questions.question: Variable decelerations on FHR tracing — first nursing action?
options: Change maternal position, stop oxytocin if running, O₂, IV fluid bolus if ordered
practice_questions.explanation: Variable decels suggest cord compression — intrauterine resuscitation: position change (side-lying), O₂, fluids, amnioinfusion if ordered.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Repetitive severe variables with late decels indicate nonreassuring tracing requiring provider escalation.

source.title: Open RN — Maternal-Newborn Nursing
source.citation: Open RN OER — Maternal-Newborn; NCSBN Health Promotion

practice_questions.question: Eclamptic seizure in labor — priority after ensuring safety?
options: Magnesium sulfate per protocol and notify provider
practice_questions.explanation: Magnesium sulfate is anticonvulsant of choice in eclampsia — monitor RR, reflexes, urine output for toxicity.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Recurrent seizures cause maternal hypoxia and fetal bradycardia — magnesium prevents recurrence.

source.title: Open RN — Maternal-Newborn Nursing
source.citation: Open RN OER — Maternal-Newborn; NCSBN Health Promotion

practice_questions.question: Placenta previa is suspected when a pregnant patient has:
options: Painless bright red bleeding in third trimester
practice_questions.explanation: Placenta previa: painless bleeding — no vaginal exams; ultrasound confirms; prepare for cesarean if complete previa.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Digital cervical exam can cause catastrophic hemorrhage in previa.

source.title: Open RN — Maternal-Newborn Nursing
source.citation: Open RN OER — Maternal-Newborn; NCSBN Health Promotion

practice_questions.question: A newborn has acrocyanosis (blue hands/feet) with pink trunk. The nurse:
options: Documents as common transitional finding if breathing and tone normal
Applies heat lamp to trunk only
practice_questions.explanation: Acrocyanosis is normal transition in first hours — assess respiratory effort, heart rate, and central color.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Central cyanosis (trunk/mucosa) is abnormal and requires immediate intervention.

source.title: Open RN — Maternal-Newborn Nursing
source.citation: Open RN OER — Maternal-Newborn; NCSBN Health Promotion

practice_questions.question: Breastfeeding mother reports cracked nipples and engorgement on day 3. Best teaching?
options: Ensure deep latch, frequent feeding, hand express for relief
Apply ice only between all feeds
practice_questions.explanation: Latch correction prevents further trauma; frequent emptying reduces engorgement; lanolin or breast milk on nipples.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Poor latch leads to mastitis, inadequate infant intake, and early weaning.

source.title: Open RN — Maternal-Newborn Nursing
source.citation: Open RN OER — Maternal-Newborn; NCSBN Health Promotion

practice_questions.question: A 2-month-old receives first routine vaccines per CDC schedule. The nurse documents:
options: Lot number, site, route, VIS date, patient reaction
practice_questions.explanation: Document each vaccine with lot, expiration, site (vastus lateralis IM for infants), and Vaccine Information Statement provided.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Accurate immunization records prevent duplicate doses and support public health tracking.

source.title: Open RN — Maternal-Newborn Nursing
source.citation: Open RN OER — Maternal-Newborn; NCSBN Health Promotion

practice_questions.question: Postpartum patient with foul-smelling lochia, temp 38.5°C, uterine tenderness — suspect:
options: Endometritis — notify provider, cultures, antibiotics per order
practice_questions.explanation: Endometritis: fever, fundal tenderness, foul lochia — usually after cesarean or prolonged labor; broad-spectrum antibiotics.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Untreated endometritis progresses to sepsis — early recognition reduces ICU admission.

source.title: Open RN — Maternal-Newborn Nursing
source.citation: Open RN OER — Maternal-Newborn; NCSBN Health Promotion

practice_questions.question: Toddler (18 months) developmental milestone expected:
practice_questions.explanation: 18-month toddler walks well, may run, uses simple words, feeds self — assess safety (gates, poison control).
practice_questions.clinical_why: Delayed walking or speech warrants early intervention referral.

source.title: Open RN — Maternal-Newborn Nursing
source.citation: Open RN OER — Maternal-Newborn; NCSBN Health Promotion

practice_questions.question: Gestational diabetes screening with 1-hour glucose challenge 165 mg/dL — next step?
options: 3-hour oral glucose tolerance test per protocol
practice_questions.explanation: Abnormal 50-g screen (typically ≥140 mg/dL) requires 100-g 3-hour OGTT for GDM diagnosis.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Untreated GDM increases macrosomia, shoulder dystocia, and neonatal hypoglycemia risk.

source.title: Open RN — Maternal-Newborn Nursing
source.citation: Open RN OER — Maternal-Newborn; NCSBN Health Promotion

practice_questions.question: Newborn of mother with active genital HSV lesions at delivery requires:
options: Cesarean delivery to reduce neonatal herpes transmission
practice_questions.explanation: Active genital HSV at delivery — cesarean recommended; neonatal herpes has high mortality even with treatment.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Neonatal herpes causes encephalitis — prevention is critical when lesions present.

source.title: Open RN — Maternal-Newborn Nursing
source.citation: Open RN OER — Maternal-Newborn; NCSBN Health Promotion

practice_questions.question: A school-age child with asthma reports daily symptoms and nighttime cough twice weekly. Classification is:
options: Moderate persistent — step-up therapy per NAEPP guidelines
practice_questions.explanation: Moderate persistent: daily symptoms, nighttime >1×/week but not nightly, FEV1 60–80% — needs daily controller.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Under-treated persistent asthma causes missed school and status asthmaticus.

source.title: Open RN — Maternal-Newborn Nursing
source.citation: Open RN OER — Maternal-Newborn; NCSBN Health Promotion

source.title: Open RN — Maternal-Newborn Nursing
source.citation: Open RN OER — Maternal-Newborn; NCSBN Health Promotion

immunization_schedule.nursing_considerations: Birth dose within 24 hr; document lot and site. Screen maternal HBsAg status.
Local reaction common; fever — antipyretic per order. Contraindicated if encephalopathy within 7 days of prior dose.
IM or SQ per product; observe 15 min post-vaccination.
Protects against Haemophilus influenzae type b meningitis/epiglottitis.
Pneumococcal conjugate — reduces otitis, pneumonia, bacteremia.
Oral vaccine; latex allergy caution with some applicators.
Live attenuated — avoid in severe immunocompromise; give VIS sheet.
Airborne + contact precautions if active varicella in susceptible contacts.
Recommended for all children; travel/endemic exposure teaching.
First season: 2 doses if <8 yr and previously unvaccinated.
Cancer prevention; counsel on syncope after injection in adolescents.
Pertussis protection wanes — adolescent booster protects infants via cocooning.
Required for college/military in many states; MenB separate series for high risk.
